text:
A single piano note falls into silence like a stone dropped into still water. Then another, and another, each one deliberate, unhurried, as if the composer had all the time in the world and wanted you to feel that too. The left hand traces a repeating figure in the bass, a slow circular motion that becomes almost hypnotic after a while, like breathing or the turning of a wheel. Over this, a melody rises that seems to be searching for something it cannot quite name. There is grief in it, but not the sharp kind. It is the grief of someone standing at a window watching rain, remembering something beautiful that is simply over.

The music never rushes. It earns each moment by taking it slowly. When it reaches what might be called a climax, it does so not through force but through accumulation, the way clouds darken before a storm not by becoming louder but by becoming more. Then it begins to recede, returning to those same spare notes at the beginning, as if the whole journey has led back to where it started, except now the silence between the notes feels different, heavier, more aware of itself.

When the last note fades, the room seems to hold its breath for a moment before the world comes back.
